content:
Hi there!
I am new at Linux Kernel programming with Rust.
I was able to set up a dev environment to start coding a kernel module, but I can't quite understand how to compile it into loadable kernel object file. I'd want to ask you for some guides and useful links.
Thank you for help in advance!

In recent mainline we have two samples at the moment (more to come hopefully soon). If you enable them to be built as modules (= M) then you should get two kernel modules "*.ko". Is this what you are looking for?

Hi!
Thanks for answering.
Ideally I'd want to compile my own module. With the info from your answer, I can replace one of them with my own module and compile it. Am I right? Or there is another easier way to compile my own module?

I've found the answer to my question. As I've find out the answer is preatty easy - compile the kernel with Rust and then compile the module with KBuild as it done here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module/

Mike said:

I've found the answer to my question. As I've find out the answer is preatty easy - compile the kernel with Rust and then compile the module with KBuild as it done here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module/

Mike, sharing some of my resources that helped my colleagues. Depending on what you'll be doing, you might need to create more bindings from the C side. I have this repository. It uses Thermal bindings from this commit/tree.

Mike said:

I've found the answer to my question. As I've find out the answer is preatty easy - compile the kernel with Rust and then compile the module with KBuild as it done here: https://github.com/Rust-for-Linux/rust-out-of-tree-module/

To clarify, that is an out-of-tree module, which is possible to use, but I normally suggest developing in-tree.

To do it in-tree, you don't need to replace an existing module, you can simply add a new one by mimicking what the others do. As @Dirk Behme mentioned, looking at the samples is an easy way to get started. You will need to modify the Kconfig and Makefile files in the folder, plus adding a new .rs file.

Hi there once again!
@Charalampos Mitrodimas I've tried method you've shown to generate bindings for netfilter.h header. I have a problem with symbols. As I can see, bindgen have generated an implementaion of the Default trait to nf_hook_ops structure. However, when I try to use it I get an error like this:
ERROR: modpost: "_RNvXsej_Cs1peCXeTTL3P_9netfilterNtB6_11nf_hook_opsNtNtCse0oTBvBC9oH_4core7default7Default7default" [/home/user/source/smth/smth.ko] undefined!

Can someone help with this, since I wasn't been able to solve this myself for quite some time

Mike said:

Hi there once again!
Charalampos Mitrodimas I've tried method you've shown to generate bindings for netfilter.h header. I have a problem with symbols. As I can see, bindgen have generated an implementaion of the Default trait to nf_hook_ops structure. However, when I try to use it I get an error like this:
ERROR: modpost: "_RNvXsej_Cs1peCXeTTL3P_9netfilterNtB6_11nf_hook_opsNtNtCse0oTBvBC9oH_4core7default7Default7default" [/home/user/source/smth/smth.ko] undefined!

Can you provide more info? A diff of how you generated the bindings maybe?
Ok, nevermind. I just needed a bit of rest and a fresh look into the code to recognize the typo in my Kbuild file
